title: Monaco taxes: what you actually pay
excerpt: Zero personal income tax has been the rule in Monaco since 1869, and it remains true today. But "no income tax" is not the same as "no taxes" — several levies still apply, and one nationality is treated differently from every other.
body_html:
Zero personal income tax has been the rule in Monaco since 1869, and it remains true today. But "no income tax" is not the same as "no taxes" — several levies still apply, and one nationality is treated differently from every other.

What is genuinely zero

For non-French residents, personal income tax, capital gains tax, wealth tax and annual property tax are all 0%. There is no equivalent of a council tax or an annual levy simply for owning a home in the Principality.

The French exception

French nationals resident in Monaco are the one group excluded from this picture. Under the 1963 Franco-Monegasque fiscal convention, they remain liable for French income tax as if they were resident in France — a detail that surprises many French buyers who assume Monaco residency itself is the tax event.

What applies to everyone

Tax / dutyRateWho pays
Registration duty on purchase (individual/SCI)4.75%Buyer
Registration duty (foreign company, disclosed)7.5%Buyer
Registration duty (non-transparent structure)Up to 10%Buyer
Leasehold duty on annual rent1%Tenant
Corporate income tax (if >25% revenue from abroad)25%Company
Inheritance/gift tax, direct line0%Heir
Inheritance/gift tax, siblings8%Heir
Inheritance/gift tax, non-relatives16%Heir
"Monaco's tax appeal isn't the absence of every tax — it's the absence of the one that compounds every year: income tax."
0%
Income & capital gains tax*
0%
Annual property tax
25%
Corporate tax, reduced 2022

*Excludes French nationals under the 1963 convention.

Two conditions, not one

To benefit from the zero-income-tax regime, an individual needs both administrative residency (the Carte de Séjour) and a separate Monaco tax residency certificate, which generally requires spending more than 183 days per year in the Principality or having one's centre of economic interests there. Buying a property does not, by itself, satisfy either requirement.
sources: Sources: Direction des Services Fiscaux (Monaco), Law No. 1.548 (2023)
disclaimer: This article is for general information only and does not constitute tax or legal advice. Individual circumstances vary — consult a qualified tax adviser before making decisions based on residency or nationality.
